channel.title: Tip on Daniels page
channel.description: Recent content in Tip on Daniels page

item.title: Favourites: Spring 2018
item.description: Here are some of my favourites from Spring 2018. www I build a twitter-bot with Cheap Bots, Done Quick. It tweets proverbs, heuristics, fallacies and biases in the wayne of the seminal work of Tversky and Kahneman, Gerd Gigerenzer & co. Ralph Hammer started an amazing series of articles about learning to draw (and by implication see). Starting with an exposition about seeing vs. reading. Now, why he considers “writing software” as a low-dopamine activity is beyond me.

item.title: Media Diet Dump
item.description: John Le Carré - The Pigeon Tunnel. Reading Carrè is always a pleasure. This one is suprisingly self-reflexive. Matthias Adolfosn. Playfull and witty illustration. Can’t get enough of them. Kosko - Neural Networks and Fuzzy Systems: An oldie. Intersting to get a grasp of the historical developement of artifical neural networks and fuzzy logic. Song of the Sea. <3 Patterson. Don’t get fooled by the trailer. The movie is very slow; almost meditative.

item.title: Using if else with pipes
item.description: A nice way to use if else within pipes is (see here): library(magrittr) runif(1) %>% magrittr::is_greater_than(0.5) %>% {if(.) "foo" else "bar"} ## [1] "foo" Alternatively, the when function from the purrr package can be used: library(purrr, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE) runif(1) %>% purrr::when(. <= 0.5 ~ "foo", . > 0.5 ~ "bar") ## [1] "bar" A nice way to use a vector of inputs is provided by the case_when function from dplyr together with mutate or transmute:

item.title: Feedback with tribbles
item.description: The ordering and display of tables in Rmarkdown (aka data.frames or tibbles) has become so good. I actually prefer it over using print right now. Especially, because I can format in in an intuitive way by using the tribble() function. Here is an example. Let us say I want to summarize the basic set up for an optimization. Some dummy variables might look like this: library(tibble) ## Warning: Paket 'tibble' wurde unter R Version 3.
